title: ï¿½none
content:
There lies a vale in Ida, lovelier
Than all the valleys of Ionian hills.
The swimming vapour slopes athwart the glen,
Puts forth an arm, and creeps from pine to pine,
And loiters, slowly drawn. On either hand
The lawns and meadow-ledges midway down
Hang rich in flowers, and far below them roars
The long brook falling throï¿½ the clovï¿½n ravine
In cataract after cataract to the sea.
Behind the valley topmost Gargarus
Stands up and takes the morning: but in front
The gorges, opening wide apart, reveal
Troas and Ilionï¿½s columnï¿½d citadel,
The crown of Troas.

Hither came at noon

Mournful ï¿½none, wandering forlorn
Of Paris, once her playmate on the hills.
Her cheek had lost the rose, and round her neck
Floated her hair or seemï¿½d to float in rest.
She, leaning on a fragment twined with vine,
Sang to the stillness, till the mountain-shade
Sloped downward to her seat from the upper cliff.


ï¿½O mother Ida, many-fountainï¿½d Ida,
Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
For now the noonday quiet holds the hill:
The grasshopper is silent in the grass:
The lizard, with his shadow on the stone,
Rests like a shadow, and the winds are dead.
The purple flower droops: the golden bee
Is lily-cradled: I alone awake.
My eyes are full of tears, my heart of love,
My heart is breaking, and my eyes are dim,
And I am all aweary of my life.


ï¿½O mother Ida, many-fountainï¿½d Ida,
Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
Hear me, O Earth, hear me, O Hills, O Caves
That house the cold crownï¿½d snake! O mountain brooks,
I am the daughter of a River-God,
Hear me, for I will speak, and build up all
My sorrow with my song, as yonder walls
Rose slowly to a music slowly breathed,
A cloud that gatherï¿½d shape: for it may be
That, while I speak of it, a little while
My heart may wander from its deeper woe.


ï¿½O mother Ida, many-fountainï¿½d Ida,
Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
I waited underneath the dawning hills,
Aloft the mountain lawn was dewy-dark,
And dewy-dark aloft the mountain pine:
Beautiful Paris, evil-hearted Paris,
Leading a jet-black goat white-hornï¿½d, white-hooved,
Came up from reedy Simois all alone.


ï¿½O mother Ida, harken ere I die.
Far-off the torrent callï¿½d me from the cleft:
Far up the solitary morning smote
The streaks of virgin snow. With down-dropt eyes
I sat alone: white-breasted like a star
Fronting the dawn he moved; a leopard skin
Droopï¿½d from his shoulder, but his sunny hair
Clusterï¿½d about his temples like a Godï¿½s:
And his cheek brightenï¿½d as the foam-bow brightens
When the wind blows the foam, and all my heart
Went forth to embrace him coming ere he came.


ï¿½Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
He smiled, and opening out his milk-white palm
Disclosed a fruit of pure Hesperian gold,
That smelt ambrosially, and while I lookï¿½d
And listenï¿½d, the full-flowing river of speech
Came down upon my heart.

ï¿½My own ï¿½none,

Beautiful-browï¿½d ï¿½none, my own soul,
Behold this fruit, whose gleaming rind ingravï¿½n
ï¿½For the most fair,ï¿½ would seem to award it thine,
As lovelier than whatever Oread haunt
The knolls of Ida, loveliest in all grace
Of movement, and the charm of married brows.ï¿½


ï¿½Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
He prest the blossom of his lips to mine,
And added ï¿½This was cast upon the board,
When all the full-faced presence of the Gods
Ranged in the halls of Peleus; whereupon
Rose feud, with question unto whom ï¿½twere due:
But light-foot Iris brought it yester-eve,
Delivering, that to me, by common voice
Elected umpire, Herï¿½ comes to-day,
Pallas and Aphroditï¿½, claiming each
This meed of fairest. Thou, within the cave
Behind yon whispering tuft of oldest pine,
Mayst well behold them unbeheld, unheard
Hear all, and see thy Paris judge of Gods.ï¿½


ï¿½Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
It was the deep midnoon: one silvery cloud
Had lost his way between the piney sides
Of this long glen. Then to the bower they came,
Naked they came to that smooth-swarded bower,
And at their feet the crocus brake like fire,
Violet, amaracus, and asphodel,
Lotos and lilies: and a wind arose,
And overhead the wandering ivy and vine,
This way and that, in many a wild festoon
Ran riot, garlanding the gnarled boughs
With bunch and berry and flower throï¿½ and throï¿½.


ï¿½O mother Ida, harken ere I die.
On the tree-tops a crested peacock lit,
And oï¿½er him flowï¿½d a golden cloud, and leanï¿½d
Upon him, slowly dropping fragrant dew.
Then first I heard the voice of her, to whom
Coming throï¿½ Heaven, like a light that grows
Larger and clearer, with one mind the Gods
Rise up for reverence. She to Paris made
Proffer of royal power, ample rule
Unquestionï¿½d, overflowing revenue
Wherewith to embellish state, ï¿½from many a vale
And river-sunderï¿½d champaign clothed with corn,
Or labourï¿½d mine undrainable of ore.
Honour,ï¿½ she said, ï¿½and homage, tax and toll,
From many an inland town and haven large,
Mast-throngï¿½d beneath her shadowing citadel
In glassy bays among her tallest towers.ï¿½


ï¿½O mother Ida, harken ere I die.
Still she spake on and still she spake of power,
ï¿½Which in all action is the end of all;
Power fitted to the season; wisdom-bred
And throned of wisdomï¿½from all neighbour crowns
Alliance and allegiance, till thy hand
Fail from the sceptre-staff. Such boon from me,
From me, Heavenï¿½s Queen, Paris, to thee king-born,
A shepherd all thy life but yet king-born,
Should come most welcome, seeing men, in power
Only, are likest Gods, who have attainï¿½d
Rest in a happy place and quiet seats
Above the thunder, with undying bliss
In knowledge of their own supremacy.ï¿½


ï¿½Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
She ceased, and Paris held the costly fruit
Out at armï¿½s-length, so much the thought of power
Flatterï¿½d his spirit; but Pallas where she stood
Somewhat apart, her clear and bared limbs
Oï¿½erthwarted with the brazen-headed spear
Upon her pearly shoulder leaning cold,
The while, above, her full and earnest eye
Over her snow-cold breast and angry cheek
Kept watch, waiting decision, made reply.


ï¿½ï¿½Self-reverence, self-knowledge, self-control,
These three alone lead life to sovereign power.
Yet not for power (power of herself
Would come uncallï¿½d for) but to live by law,
Acting the law we live by without fear;
And, because right is right, to follow right
Were wisdom in the scorn of consequence.ï¿½


ï¿½Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
Again she said: ï¿½I woo thee not with gifts.
Sequel of guerdon could not alter me
To fairer. Judge thou me by what I am,
So shalt thou find me fairest.

Yet, indeed,

If gazing on divinity disrobed
Thy mortal eyes are frail to judge of fair,
Unbiasï¿½d by self-profit, oh! rest thee sure
That I shall love thee well and cleave to thee,
So that my vigour, wedded to thy blood,
Shall strike within thy pulses, like a Godï¿½s,
To push thee forward throï¿½ a life of shocks,
Dangers, and deeds, until endurance grow
Sinewï¿½d with action, and the full-grown will,
Circled throï¿½ all experiences, pure law,
Commeasure perfect freedom.ï¿½

ï¿½Here she ceasï¿½d,

And Paris ponderï¿½d, and I cried, ï¿½O Paris,
Give it to Pallas!ï¿½ but he heard me not,
Or hearing would not hear me, woe is me!


ï¿½O mother Ida, many-fountainï¿½d Ida,
Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
Idalian Aphroditï¿½ beautiful,
Fresh as the foam, new-bathed in Paphian wells,
With rosy slender fingers backward drew
From her warm brows and bosom her deep hair
Ambrosial, golden round her lucid throat
And shoulder: from the violets her light foot
Shone rosy-white, and oï¿½er her rounded form
Between the shadows of the vine-bunches
Floated the glowing sunlights, as she moved.


ï¿½Dear mother Ida, harken ere I die.
She with a subtle smile in her mild eyes,
The herald of her triumph, drawing nigh
Half-whisperï¿½d in his ear, ï¿½I promise thee
The fairest and most loving wife in Greece.ï¿½
She spoke and laughï¿½d: I shut my sight for fear:
But when I lookï¿½d, Paris had raised his arm,
And I beheld great Herï¿½s angry eyes,
As she withdrew into the golden cloud,
And I was left alone within the bower;
And from that time to this I am alone,
And I shall be alone until I die.


ï¿½Yet, mother Ida, harken ere I die.
Fairestï¿½why fairest wife? am I not fair?
My love hath told me so a thousand times.
Methinks I must be fair, for yesterday,
When I past by, a wild and wanton pard,
Eyed like the evening star, with playful tail
Crouchï¿½d fawning in the weed. Most loving is she?
Ah me, my mountain shepherd, that my arms
Were wound about thee, and my hot lips prest
Close, close to thine in that quick-falling dew
Of fruitful kisses, thick as Autumn rains
Flash in the pools of whirling Simois.


ï¿½O mother, hear me yet before I die.
They came, they cut away my tallest pines,
My tall dark pines, that plumed the craggy ledge
High over the blue gorge, and all between
The snowy peak and snow-white cataract
Fosterï¿½d the callow eagletï¿½from beneath
Whose thick mysterious boughs in the dark morn
The pantherï¿½s roar came muffled, while I sat
Low in the valley. Never, never more
Shall lone ï¿½none see the morning mist
Sweep throï¿½ them; never see them overlaid
With narrow moon-lit slips of silver cloud,
Between the loud stream and the trembling stars.


ï¿½O mother, hear me yet before I die.
I wish that somewhere in the ruinï¿½d folds,
Among the fragments tumbled from the glens,
Or the dry thickets, I could meet with her
The Abominable, that uninvited came
Into the fair Peleï¿½an banquet-hall,
And cast the golden fruit upon the board,
And bred this change; that I might speak my mind,
And tell her to her face how much I hate
Her presence, hated both of Gods and men.


ï¿½O mother, hear me yet before I die.
Hath he not sworn his love a thousand times,
In this green valley, under this green hill,
Evï¿½n on this hand, and sitting on this stone?
Sealï¿½d it with kisses? waterï¿½d it with tears?
O happy tears, and how unlike to these!
O happy Heaven, how canst thou see my face?
O happy earth, how canst thou bear my weight?
O death, death, death, thou ever-floating cloud,
There are enough unhappy on this earth,
Pass by the happy souls, that love to live:
I pray thee, pass before my light of life,
And shadow all my soul, that I may die.
Thou weighest heavy on the heart within,
Weigh heavy on my eyelids: let me die.


ï¿½O mother, hear me yet before I die.
I will not die alone, for fiery thoughts
Do shape themselves within me, more and more,
Whereof I catch the issue, as I hear
Dead sounds at night come from the inmost hills,
Like footsteps upon wool. I dimly see
My far-off doubtful purpose, as a mother
Conjectures of the features of her child
Ere it is born: her child!ï¿½a shudder comes
Across me: never child be born of me,
Unblest, to vex me with his fatherï¿½s eyes!


ï¿½O mother, hear me yet before I die.
Hear me, O earth. I will not die alone,
Lest their shrill happy laughter come to me
Walking the cold and starless road of Death
Uncomforted, leaving my ancient love
With the Greek woman. I will rise and go
Down into Troy, and ere the stars come forth
Talk with the wild Cassandra, for she says
A fire dances before her, and a sound
Rings ever in her ears of armed men.
What this may be I know not, but I know
That, wheresoeï¿½er I am by night and day,
All earth and air seem only burning fire.ï¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alfred Lord Tennyson represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alfred Lord Tennyson's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.
